World Bank Group·ALASA
|
Carlos Aguilar · MIA
v3.5 · June 2026

Agricultural insurance in Latin America & the Caribbean

Official indicators of agricultural insurance across nine priority countries, compiled directly from each country's insurance supervisor, agriculture ministry and statistical office. Seven markets publish a full agricultural line: Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Honduras, Mexico and Peru. Ecuador appears through its whole insurance market while its agricultural program remains unpublished, and Jamaica reports no agricultural insurance class at all.

01 · Overview

Regional MAP by indicator and year

The regional map: every indicator in the system, country by country, one year at a time.
Details
Pick a data group and then an indicator chip to draw it on the map; the year control (arrows, slider or play) moves the whole pane and opens on the year with the broadest coverage for the chosen indicator. Clicking a country, on the map or on its chip, pins its values below. The groups cover the agricultural-insurance line, the whole insurance market, and the agricultural context behind the denominators.
Year 2025

Selected country · click a country to mirror
02 · Agricultural insurance · comparable

Agricultural insurance · comparable indicators for every country

The indicators that can be read side by side across countries: insured area, the two take-up rates, claims paid, the physical loss ratio, and agriculture's share of each insurance market.
Details
Every row ranks the countries that report the indicator in the selected year; clicking a row focuses the country chart and the multi-country trend below, and the year control opens on the year with the widest coverage for the focused indicator. Coverage follows what each source publishes: the take-up rates exist where insured area and cropland are both official (Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil and Colombia), the physical loss ratio only for Bolivia, and everything else spans the seven countries with a published agricultural line.
Year 2025
03 · Agricultural insurance · commercial

Agricultural insurance · commercial-market depth and structure

The commercial economics of the agricultural line: premium, claims, loss and combined ratios, reinsurance cession, sum insured, and market concentration.
Details
Concentration is the Herfindahl index of per-company premium, possible where the supervisor publishes a per-company split (Argentina, Brazil, Colombia, Honduras and Peru); Bolivia's single commercial insurer makes the index structural, and Mexico's open data has no per-company split. Bolivia's small commercial line is shown alongside its state catastrophic program. Every monetary series is expressed in constant 2015 US dollars, converted year by year.
Year 2025
04 · Structural contrast

What the numbers actually compare

The seven markets are built differently; this table and chart keep that structure in view before any cross-country comparison.
Details
In commercial markets, producers pay the premium; in state catastrophic programs, the state pays it on behalf of smallholders; several markets mix both. The table classifies each market. The chart places all of them, at one selected year, on agriculture's share of the total insurance market (penetration) against the loss ratio (underwriting risk), with bubble size following premium volume; a market drops out of the chart if it lacks either input in the selected year.
Year 2025
Market archetype · all insurance-line countries
How each market is structured
Commercial = producers pay premium; state-catastrophic = the state pays a subsidized premium for smallholders.
Ecuador and Jamaica are not classified here: neither publishes an agricultural insurance line to structure.
Penetration vs underwriting risk · latest year
Where each market sits
Farm insurance as % of all insurance (how large the ag line is within the country's insurance market) against the loss ratio (claims vs premiums; underwriting risk). Bubble size scales with premiums paid. Every market is shown at the same selected year; a market drops out if it lacks either input that year.
07 · Subnational

Subnational drill-down by country

-
Departmental take-up · campaign 2023-24
Take-up rate by department
Insured hectares / INE departmental cropland x 100. Beni and Pando absent from INSA Cuadro 4 (no INSA presence).
Source: INSA Compendio Cuadro 4 + INE departmental cropland.
Departmental cropland and insured hectares · campaign 2023-24
Cropland vs insured area by department
Santa Cruz dominates cropland (3.3 M ha, log scale) but INSA reach is minimal there; INSA is concentrated in the highland departments.
Source: INE 2023-24 + INSA Cuadro 4.
Per-crop take-up · 12 campaigns
Take-up rate by crop
% of INE national crop hectares insured under INSA catastrophic, year-matched per campaign. Limited to the 7 INSA focus crops.
Source: INSA Compendio Figuras "Cobertura por cultivo" + INE national crop hectares.
Per-crop insured hectares · 12 campaigns
Insured area by crop
Stacked hectares insured per campaign. Papa and Maíz form the bulk; combined seven focus crops account for the catastrophic program.
Source: INSA Compendio Figuras 2/24/48/72/97/122/144/169/188/210/235/260.
Insurer solvency · whole-company · reference
Agricultural-line insurers: solvency ratio
Available capital divided by required capital, per insurer (1.0 = exactly meets the requirement), latest year. A whole-company figure across all insurance lines, not agriculture-specific; each regulator defines solvency its own way, so this is a within-country reference, not comparable across countries.
Source: APS Anuarios de Seguros, Margen de Solvencia y Patrimonio Técnico por Entidad Aseguradora.
State take-up · most recent year reported
Take-up rate by state
Paraná and the southern states lead; the largest-cropland states such as Mato Grosso sit low because their cropland base dwarfs the subsidy program.
Source: MAPA SISSER insured hectares + IBGE PAM cropland (2024).
State cropland and insured hectares · 2024
Cropland vs insured area by state
Mato Grosso, Paraná, and Rio Grande do Sul dominate cropland; insurance reach is concentrated in the southern states (Paraná, Rio Grande do Sul, Santa Catarina, Minas Gerais).
Source: IBGE PAM + MAPA SISSER, 2024.
Per-crop take-up · 2010 to 2024
Take-up rate by crop
Federally-subsidized insured hectares per focus crop divided by IBGE national area planted for that crop, year-matched.
Source: MAPA SISSER (by crop, aggregated from policy micro-data) + IBGE PAM national area planted.
Per-crop insured hectares · 2010 to 2024
Insured area by crop
Stacked hectares insured per year. Soybean and maize dominate the program throughout. Maize and beans are written per safra (season); their safras are summed here.
Source: MAPA SISSER by-crop aggregation from policy-level records.
Insurer solvency · whole-company · reference
Agricultural-line insurers: solvency ratio
Available capital divided by required capital, per insurer (1.0 = exactly meets the requirement), latest year (top 15 shown). A whole-company figure across all insurance lines, not agriculture-specific; each regulator defines solvency its own way, so this is a within-country reference, not comparable across countries.
Source: SUSEP SES, Ses_pl_margem (Patrimônio Líquido Ajustado / Capital Mínimo Requerido) by company.
Take-up by department · 2023
Take-up rate by department
FINAGRO insured hectares divided by UPRA EVA cropland by department, year-matched. Caldas reflects the heavily-insured coffee belt; Sucre and Córdoba reflect the rice and livestock corridors of the Caribbean. Meta and Casanare (the largest cropland states) sit lower because their cropland base dwarfs the federal subsidy. FINAGRO-insured hectares can exceed the EVA cropland denominator (multi-season policies; EVA undercount), so take-up can exceed 100% (Vichada 2021-2022).
Source: FINAGRO ISA + UPRA EVA, 2023.
Cropland and insured area by department · 2023
Cropland vs insured area by department
Per-department cropland from the UPRA Evaluaciones Agropecuarias Municipales versus FINAGRO subsidized insured hectares. Meta dominates cropland; insurance reach is concentrated in the coffee and Caribbean departments.
Source: UPRA EVA + FINAGRO ISA, 2023.
Take-up rate by crop · 2019 to 2024
Take-up rate by crop
FINAGRO insured hectares divided by national area for each focus crop, year-matched.
Source: FINAGRO ISA + UPRA EVA, by crop.
Insured area by crop · 2015 to 2024
Insured area by crop
Stacked hectares insured per year, by crop. The portfolio has tilted toward rice and coffee over the decade.
Source: FINAGRO ISA microdata by crop.
Federal premium subsidy · 2015 to 2024
Federal premium subsidy, over time
Total federal subsidy paid under the ISA program each year, in nominal Colombian pesos. The 2023-2024 jump reflects the small-producer expansion.
Source: FINAGRO ISA microdata, sum of subsidy across all policies.
Insurer solvency · whole-company · reference
Agricultural-line insurers: solvency ratio
Available capital divided by required capital, per insurer (1.0 = exactly meets the requirement), latest year. A whole-company figure across all insurance lines, not agriculture-specific; each regulator defines solvency its own way, so this is a within-country reference, not comparable across countries.
Source: SFC Formato 407 (Total Patrimonio Técnico / Total Patrimonio Adecuado), datos.gov.co.
Credit access by department · census 2013
Credit access by department (census)
Share of all census production units whose credit application was approved during 2013 (DANE, 3er Censo Nacional Agropecuario 2014; national 7.7%). Nariño (13.6%), Huila (13.5%) and Norte de Santander (10.8%) lead. The census asked about credit only, there is no insurance question. Unit-based: not comparable to the hectare-based take-up rates.
Source: DANE, 3er Censo Nacional Agropecuario 2014, módulo Unidad productora.
Agricultural premium by state · 2024
Agricultural premium by state
Agropecuario premium issued by entidad, latest year, constant 2015 US dollars. The Pacific-northwest irrigated belt (Sinaloa, Sonora) and the Bajío (Guanajuato, Jalisco) dominate.
Source: CNSF SESA agropecuario, premium by entidad, 2024.
Agricultural premium by state · 2015 to 2024
Premium by state, over time
Stacked premium by the eight largest states (rest grouped as "Others"), constant 2015 US dollars. The national line fell about 73% over the decade with the post-2019 cut to the CADENA catastrophic program and premium subsidies.
Source: CNSF SESA agropecuario, premium by entidad.
Sum insured by state · 2024
Sum insured by state
Suma asegurada by entidad, latest year, constant 2015 US dollars. Reflects where the largest insured values sit, distinct from premium because rates differ by crop and peril.
Source: CNSF SESA agropecuario, sum insured by entidad, 2024.
Premium and sum insured by state · 2024
Premium vs sum insured by state
The largest states by sum insured, showing premium and sum insured side by side on a log scale. The gap between the two is the implied average rate-on-line.
Source: CNSF SESA agropecuario, 2024.
Insurance access by state · census 2022
Insurance access by state (census)
Share of active production units that obtained agricultural or livestock insurance, INEGI census (October 2021 to September 2022). Sinaloa (32.3%), Baja California (18.9%) and Sonora (16.8%) lead a national share of just 1.9%. Unit-based and demand-side: not comparable to the hectare-based take-up rates.
Source: INEGI, Censo Agropecuario 2022 (cuadro ca2022_cred01).
Credit access by state · census 2022
Credit access by state (census)
Share of active production units that obtained a credit or loan for agricultural activity (national 6.1%). The census asks credit and insurance in the same question block, so the two maps are directly comparable.
Source: INEGI, Censo Agropecuario 2022 (cuadro ca2022_cred01).
Access by farm size · census 2022
Insurance and credit access by farm size (census)
Share of active production units that obtained insurance and credit, by total-surface stratum (national). Access climbs from 0.3% of units up to 2 hectares to 7.5% at 100 to 1,000 hectares, then dips for the largest strata, consistent with self-insurance. The smallholder exclusion in one ladder.
Source: INEGI, Censo Agropecuario 2022 (cuadro ca2022_str_cred01).
Production losses by state · census 2022
Loss incidence by state (census)
Share of production units that reported production losses in the census cycle (October 2021 to September 2022; among the 79% of units that answered the module). National: 61.0% suffered losses, and of those only 1.4% received economic support for the catastrophe.
Source: INEGI, Censo Agropecuario 2022 (cuadro ca2022_prob01).
What causes the losses · census 2022
Climate loss factors (census, national)
Among units with climate-driven losses, the share citing each factor (multi-answer, so shares sum to more than 100). Drought dominates at 72.2%; 88.3% of all loss-affected units cite climatic factors. The Mexican parallel to Bolivia’s damage-by-peril panel.
Source: INEGI, Censo Agropecuario 2022 (cuadro ca2022_prob01).
SAC premium budget by department · campaign 2021-2022
SAC premium budget by department
Fondo disponible by departamento, constant 2015 US dollars, latest campaign. Puno, Ayacucho, and Apurímac lead, reflecting the program's focus on smallholder highland agriculture exposed to drought and frost.
Source: MIDAGRI/FOGASA RM 0271-2021 Directiva Anexo 02 (24 departamentos = S/80M).
Allocation by department · 2019-2020 vs 2021-2022
Budget by department: 2019-2020 vs 2021-2022
Fondo disponible by departamento for both published campaigns, constant 2015 US dollars. The program grew from fourteen departments (S/45.2M) to twenty-four (S/80M); the bars show which departments were added and how existing allocations changed.
Source: MIDAGRI/FOGASA RM 0447-2019 Anexo 01 + RM 0271-2021 Directiva Anexo 02.
Insurer solvency · whole-company · reference
Agricultural-line insurers: solvency ratio
Available capital divided by required capital, per insurer (1.0 = exactly meets the requirement), latest year. A whole-company figure across all insurance lines, not agriculture-specific; each regulator defines solvency its own way, so this is a within-country reference, not comparable across countries.
Source: SBS form S-332 (Patrimonio Efectivo Total / Total Requerimientos Patrimoniales) by company.
Insurer solvency · whole-company · reference
Agricultural-line insurers: solvency ratio
Available capital divided by required capital, per insurer (1.0 = exactly meets the requirement), latest year. Argentina publishes this only as a ratio (the surplus of available over required capital); the raw peso amounts are not open data. A whole-company figure across all insurance lines, not agriculture-specific; not comparable across countries. Argentina has no other published subnational insurance breakdown.
Source: SSN datos abiertos, indicadores patrimoniales (Superávit sobre capital requerido); ag insurers per SSN Cuadro 10.
Sum insured by department · latest year
Sum insured by department
Capital asegurado of the commercial agricultural line by department, latest year, constant 2015 US dollars. The market is small and geographically concentrated.
Source: CNBS, sum insured (pólizas MONTO) by department.
Sum insured by department · 2021 to 2025
Sum insured by department, over time
Stacked capital asegurado by department per year, constant 2015 US dollars.
Source: CNBS, sum insured by department.
Cropland by province · latest year · context only
Cropland by province
Planted area by province, latest year. Ecuador has no subnational insurance data (AgroSeguro is published only behind a Tableau wall), so this is the agricultural base, not insurance reach.
Source: SIPA / ESPAC, planted area by province.
Cropland by crop · 2002 to 2025 · context only
Cropland by crop, over time
Stacked planted area by crop per year. Cacao, rice and maize lead the coastal base.
Source: SIPA / ESPAC, planted area by crop.
Cropland by crop · latest year · context only
Cropland by crop
Area reaped by crop, latest year. Jamaica has no farmer-level agricultural insurance (structural; the FSC reports no agricultural class), so this is the agricultural base, not insurance reach.
Source: Ministry of Agriculture Data Bank, area reaped by crop.
Cropland by crop · 2014 to 2023 · context only
Cropland by crop, over time
Stacked area reaped by the eight largest crops per year (rest grouped as "Others").
Source: Ministry of Agriculture Data Bank, area reaped by crop.
05 · Whole insurance industry & macro

The whole insurance industry · four headline indicators, all lines

Each insurance market as a whole: total premium across every line of business, premium per person, premium against GDP, and the number of insurers writing agricultural cover.
Details
Eight countries report these whole-market indicators, Ecuador included. Every row ranks the reporting countries at the selected year; clicking a row unfolds its multi-country trend in place (click again to fold it).
Year 2024
06 · Proprietary indices

Three proprietary indices for cross-country analysis

Three composite indices condense the verified indicators into one score per country: market development, catastrophe and underwriting risk, and market structure.
Details
Each component is scaled from 0 to 100 across the scored countries and the three components are averaged with equal weights, so a score is a relative position within the group rather than an absolute level. The year control reads the level components (premium per hectare, take-up, market share, concentration, insurer count) at the selected year, with 2023 as the published reference; the risk statistics (loss-ratio mean and volatility, mean cession) are computed over the trailing five years ending at the selected year, so the Catastrophe/Risk index reads each market's current solidity and old catastrophe years roll out of it. Mexico is not scored on market structure because no per-company source exists. The component detail and the full sensitivity analysis live in the companion indices workbook.
Year 2023
Index 1 · higher = more developed
Market Development Index
Premium per cropland hectare, take-up over cropland, and agricultural market share.
Equal weights; min-max 0-100 across the scored countries.
Index 2 · higher = more exposed
Catastrophe / Underwriting Risk Exposure Index
Claims vs premiums (loss ratio) averaged over the trailing five years, its volatility (coefficient of variation) over the same window, and the mean share passed to reinsurers. A rolling window: old catastrophe years leave the index as they leave the window.
Equal weights; min-max 0-100 across the scored countries.
Index 3 · higher = more concentrated
Market Structure / Concentration Index
Herfindahl index of per-company agricultural premium and the inverted insurer count. Mexico is not scored (no per-company source); Bolivia is a structural monopoly.
Equal weights; min-max 0-100 across the scored countries.
Scorecard
Scores (0-100) and ranks
Each country's score and rank on the three indices. A dash means the country is not scored (no comparable data).
Robustness: leave-one-component-out and a 2,000-draw random-weight ensemble; see the workbook.
08 · Methodology & caveats

Assumptions, proxies, and data limitations

How the numbers are built, and where their limits are.
Details
Four cards below: the currency convention behind every monetary series, the documented workarounds that stand in for missing series, the gaps that cannot be closed from public sources, and the official publications feeding each country. The full methodology document travels with the deliverables.
Currency convention
All cross-country monetary values in USD const 2015
Method: every local-currency value is converted at its own year's official exchange rate (World Bank series PA.NUS.FCRF) and then deflated with that year's US CPI, landing every monetary figure in constant 2015 US dollars. Converting year by year, rather than at a single reporting-date rate, keeps early-year levels and growth rates undistorted through inflation and devaluations. Ratio indicators (loss ratio, cession, market share, take-up) involve no currency at all.

CPI sources: World Bank Open Data (FP.CPI.TOTL) for eight of the nine countries, with the IMF World Economic Outlook as fallback. Argentina uses an IMF plus INDEC chain because the World Bank does not publish an Argentine CPI series.
Documented proxies
Where a workaround stands in for a missing series
Bolivia's combined ratio, 2022 to 2024: the APS does not publish expenses by line of business, so the ratio uses the expenses and production of UNIBIENES, the only insurer writing agricultural cover since 2020, at whole-company level. Agriculture is roughly 1.3% of UNIBIENES's book, so the proxy describes the company's economics rather than the line's, and it is flagged wherever it appears.

Argentina's claims paid, 2010: the SSN survey series starts in 2011, so the 2010 value is reconstructed as premium times loss ratio, both taken from the evolution table of the SSN's own 2025 edition.

Take-up is always shown as two indicators, never one. The share of cropland insured uses FAOSTAT's physical cropland, where each hectare counts once a year and countries compare cleanly. The share of planted area insured uses each country's national planted-area statistics (INE for Bolivia, PAM for Brazil, EVA for Colombia, ESPAC for Ecuador), where a hectare sown twice counts twice. Brazil in 2023 shows why both matter: 9.3% of cropland insured but 6.1% of planted area, the gap being the roughly 32 million hectares of the safrinha second harvest. For Bolivia, whose catastrophic program charges producers no premium, both rates read hectares covered rather than premium-based penetration.
Structural gaps
Indicators absent at the source
Argentina's physical loss ratio: the SSN reports the amount and count of claims but not the hectares damaged, and in a market that is 97% hail cover the physical measure would say little anyway.

Bolivia's sum insured: the state program pays a fixed indemnity rather than insuring a declared capital, so there is no sum insured to report, and the APS yearbooks do not isolate it for the commercial line.

Ecuador's agricultural line: the whole insurance market is loaded from the SCVS, but the AgroSeguro program publishes its agricultural figures only inside a dashboard with no data export; a formal information request is the path to them. Until then, Ecuador contributes whole-market premium, penetration and per-capita figures.

Jamaica: the Financial Services Commission reports no agricultural or crop class. Agricultural disaster risk runs through the sovereign parametric layer (CCRIF SPC) rather than farmer-level policies, so Jamaica appears with its agricultural context and the AgriCare life and health scheme, which is social protection rather than crop insurance.
Sources currently loaded
What feeds the dashboard
Argentina: SSN Encuesta de Seguros Agropecuarios y Forestales (15 annual editions 2011-2025).

Bolivia: INSA Compendio Estadístico del Seguro Agrario 2012-2025 (catastrophic) + APS Anuarios 2010-2024 (commercial AGR) + INE crop statistics.

Brazil: SUSEP SES (whole market, ramo RURAL) + MAPA SISSER (PSR subsidy) + IBGE PAM / Censo Agropecuario.

Colombia: SFC Formato 290 (financial line) + FINAGRO ISA microdata (subsidy, hectares) + UPRA EVA (cropland).

Ecuador: SCVS ranking (whole market) + SIPA / ESPAC (cropland, credit, climate). Agricultural line pending a formal information request; the AgroSeguro program publishes no data export.

Honduras: CNBS open data (commercial ramo Agrícola) + INE crop area.

Mexico: CNSF SESA (ramo Agrícola y de Animales) + FES (whole market) + AGROASEMEX (subsidy).

Peru: SBS (ramo Agrícola, whole market) + MIDAGRI/FOGASA SAC (state catastrophic, by departamento).

FAOSTAT (7 domains, 9 countries, 2010-2024) + World Bank macro (population, GDP) + CPI deflators (WB FP.CPI.TOTL + IMF WEO + INDEC for ARG). All monetary values are in constant 2015 US dollars, converted year by year as described under the currency convention.

World Bank Group · ALASA Partnership

Every figure in this dashboard traces to the official institutions listed under Methodology & caveats, and every monetary value is expressed in constant 2015 US dollars. The data refreshes as each supervisor publishes.
